About.contributeArticlesDescription: Draft, preview, and submit articles through the built-in editor.
About.contributeBody: GTMC is open to contributions. You can draft and submit articles directly from the site, suggest topics via feature requests, or contribute to the codebase.
About.contributeCodeDescription: The website is open source. Bug reports, improvements, and new features are welcome.
About.licenseArticles: Article content is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.
About.licenseCode: Website code is licensed under Apache-2.0.
About.metaDescription: Graduate Texts in Minecraft (GTMC) is a community-written open textbook on technical Minecraft — redstone, game mechanics, and engine internals.
About.missionBody: Graduate Texts in Minecraft (GTMC) is an open knowledge base for the technical side of Minecraft. We publish tutorials, mechanics explanations, and annotated source-code readings — all written and reviewed by the community.
About.missionBody2: The project models itself on an academic monograph series: rigorous, structured content that covers the full stack of technical play, from production builds and redstone engineering to tick-level engine internals.
About.teamDescription: Articles are written by community members with hands-on expertise in technical Minecraft. Each contributor brings deep knowledge of specific subsystems — from redstone timing to chunk loading internals.

EditorialPolicy.metaDescription: How Graduate Texts in Minecraft reviews submissions, governs its editorial board, and licenses content.
EditorialPolicy.pageDescription: Review process, editorial board, community standards, and licensing
EditorialPolicy.sectionEditorialBoardBody: GTMC is governed by a single Editorial Board. Members are collectively referred to as Editors (also called Article Editors).
EditorialPolicy.sectionEditorialBoardBody2: The board runs on a hybrid seat model. Permanent seats (such as Petris and void) hold core personnel responsible for architectural decisions, arbitration of major disputes, and system revisions. Promotions to permanent seats are currently by invitation only. Rotating seats are filled by active editors selected periodically from the candidate pool; any contributor who has submitted at least one PR may be invited to the rotation candidate pool.
EditorialPolicy.sectionEditorialBoardBody3: Each rotation cycle lasts one month and selects three active editors via a weighted random process. Participation is optional — members choose whether to serve a given cycle based on their schedule. A resting principle prevents editors from serving consecutive cycles unless an extreme shortage requires it. A strict recusal policy applies: any content involving an active editor must be cross-reviewed by other board members. When an author disputes an editor's rejection or penalty, permanent-seat members intervene to make the final ruling.
EditorialPolicy.sectionLicenseBody: Article content in Graduate Texts in Minecraft is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 — the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International license.
EditorialPolicy.sectionLicenseBody2: You may share and adapt the articles for non-commercial purposes, provided you give appropriate attribution and distribute your contributions under the same license. Website code is licensed separately under Apache-2.0.
EditorialPolicy.sectionStandardsBody: All submissions must avoid factual errors. Fabricating unverified game mechanics or making severe logical errors in derivations is strictly prohibited. Minor formatting or tone issues are corrected with a reminder rather than a penalty. Academic fraud and copyright infringement — including plagiarism and unauthorized copying of others' work — are strictly forbidden.
EditorialPolicy.sectionStandardsBody2: Contributors must maintain an objective, professional tone, prioritizing neutral phrasing such as "results indicate" or "experiments show," and avoid subjective vocabulary such as "I," "we," "feels like," or "probably." All abbreviations must be spelled out and explained on first appearance, and terminology must remain consistent throughout the document. Original work must always be cited prominently.
EditorialPolicy.sectionSubmissionBody: GTMC uses a flat organizational model with two roles: reviewers and writers. Outside contributors and writers receive identical treatment, and reviewers rotate from within the writer team. Reviewers are responsible for enforcing writing standards, performing fact checks, resolving conflicts on pull requests, and approving PRs.
EditorialPolicy.sectionSubmissionBody2: To submit, draft an article on the website or locally, fill out the frontmatter at the top of your Markdown file, write using standard Markdown plus supported custom syntax, and open a Pull Request through the website or GitHub. The site opens and syncs the PR for you when you draft on the website.
EditorialPolicy.sectionSubmissionBody3: Reviewers approve, request changes, or close the PR. Once a reviewer approves, the article is merged and goes live on the next site deploy. A strict recusal policy applies: reviewers cannot approve content they submitted or primarily authored — it must be cross-reviewed by other members of the reviewer pool.
EditorialPolicy.sectionTranslationBody: Articles exist as source files paired with optional translations. Source articles own the slug and primary metadata; translations link back to their source via a translates field and pin a specific source revision through the translated-from-revision field.
EditorialPolicy.sectionTranslationBody2: When the source has moved beyond the pinned revision, the website surfaces a "translation may be stale" hint so readers can judge freshness. Translations follow the same review and recusal policies as source articles.
EditorialPolicy.sectionViolationsBody: Violations of the Code of Conduct are handled in three tiers. Level 1 (minor formatting or tone violations): a reminder is issued and the contributor is asked to revise the content to meet the guidelines, with no point deductions.
EditorialPolicy.sectionViolationsBody2: Level 2 (spamming or unauthorized copying): the violating content is deleted immediately, the contributor faces a short-term ban, and contribution points are deducted based on the circumstances. Level 3 (malicious fabrication of mechanics or personal attacks): the incident is publicly announced site-wide and the offending individual is permanently banned. A contributor who disagrees with a reviewer's rejection or factual-error penalty may appeal to core permanent members of the management team for final ruling and arbitration.

Glossary.editorAttributionBody: Your commit will be authored under: <authorName>{name}</authorName> <authorEmail>{email}</authorEmail>. By default we use your GitHub no-reply email to keep your real address private.
Glossary.editorAttributionWarning: Your GitHub identity ({name} <{email}>) will be recorded in the PR.
Glossary.editorComplexChangesBody: Working with many terms or complex changes? Clone the <repoLink>glossary repository</repoLink> and submit a PR via your git client. The web editor is ideal for small, targeted changes.
Glossary.editorComplexChangesBodyPlain: Working with many terms or complex changes? Clone the glossary repository and submit a PR via your git client. The web editor is ideal for small, targeted changes.
Glossary.editorComplexChangesNotice: For complex changes, edit directly at {repoUrl}.
Glossary.editorPrOwnershipBody: Once submitted, the PR is opened on your behalf by the gtmc-web bot. You will not be able to push further commits to it via this editor — any follow-up changes must be discussed in the PR comments on GitHub or submitted as a new draft.
Glossary.editorPrOwnershipNotice: You will own the PR and receive notifications.
Glossary.editorRealEmailToggleLabel: Use my real email instead for this submission
Glossary.pageDescription: Browse technical Minecraft terms, definitions, and translations across 12 languages.

Review.allFilesClean: All files are clean. Select a mode to proceed.
Review.conflictInstructions: Resolve conflicts in the editor below, then save to continue.
Review.mergeMethodDirectDesc: Fast-forward main to the PR head when the branch is already linear.
Review.mergeMethodDirectDetail: Best for short branches where preserving the original commit chain is more useful than rewriting it.
Review.mergeMethodDirectNote: Direct landing fast-forwards main to the pull request head. No merge commit is created and the branch commit chain is preserved as-is.
Review.mergeMethodRebaseDesc: Replay the branch commits onto main without a merge commit.
Review.mergeMethodRebaseDetail: Best when the branch history is already meaningful and should remain inspectable.
Review.mergeMethodRebaseNote: Rebase merge keeps the branch commit sequence but rewrites it onto the latest main branch tip without creating a merge commit.
Review.mergeMethodSquashDesc: Compress the pull request into one commit on main.
Review.mergeMethodSquashDetail: Best when you want a clean linear history and a curated final commit message.
Review.modeFineGrainedDetail: Higher precision. Preserves commit history context.
Review.modeSimpleDesc: Direct comparison. Single merge between your draft and current main.
Review.modeSimpleDetail: Faster resolution. Best for small or isolated changes.
